Precum un Shiba Inu adorabil care îşi aşteaptă stăpânul, fie ploaie, fie vânt, Elias Charalmambous speră încă să primească nişte indicaţii de la Becali care nu vor mai veni niciodată. Astăzi, Chambarlabous a fost văzut într-un restaurant din Capitală, vizibil înfometat, privind în […] The post Charalambous stă de 4 ore într-un restaurant şi aşteaptă să-i zică Becali ce să comande appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: S-au pus în vânzare biletele pentru Festivalul Filmului Francez .30
romania.summary: Filme de la Cannes, Berlin, Locarno, Veneția și producții premiate cu César vor fi prezentate în selecția Festivalului Filmului Francez 2026 Programul complet al ediției aniversare poate fi descoperit pe:… Articolul S-au pus în vânzare biletele pentru Festivalul Filmului Francez .30 apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: 😂 Primăvară superbă în București! Au ieșit ghioceii în miile de gropi din asfalt
romania.summary: După o iarnă lungă, Bucureștiul prinde din nou viață: păsărelele ciripesc, copacii înmuguresc, iar șoferii trag stânga-dreapta de volan ca să evite „craterele” din asfalt, care au apărut, aparent, peste noapte. Printre ele au răsărit și ghiocei! Singurele flori suficient de rezistente ca să supraviețuiască claxoanelor de taximetriști, sării de deszăpezire și promisiunilor electorale. Specialiștii […] The post Primăvară superbă în București! Au ieșit ghioceii în miile de gropi din asfalt appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Inteligența implică o coordonare a întregului creier, dezvăluie un studiu
romania.summary: Cercetătorii de la Universitatea Notre Dame sugerează că inteligența nu locuiește deloc într-o singură regiune „inteligentă” a creierului. În schimb, ea emerge din modul eficient și flexibil în care numeroasele rețele ale creierului comunică și […] Articolul Inteligența implică o coordonare a întregului creier, dezvăluie un studiu apare prima dată în Descopera .

romania.title: 😂 Românii cu Oltcit răsuflă ușurați! S-au scumpit benzina și motorina, dar împinsul e tot gratis
romania.summary: Vestea că românii vor scoate mai mulți bani din buzunar pentru deplasările cu mașina personală a creat panică printre șoferi, inclusiv printre cei care au Oltcit. Aceștia din urmă pot însă să răsufle ușurați: într-adevăr, benzina și motorina se vor scumpi, dar împinsul Oltcitului va rămâne în continuare gratuit. Chiar și așa, românii cu Oltcit […] The post Românii cu Oltcit răsuflă ușurați! S-au scumpit benzina și motorina, dar împinsul e tot gratis appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: Test de cultură generală. Apa de ploaie este bună de băut?
romania.summary: Te-ai întrebat vreodată dacă apa de ploaie este bună de băut? Răspunsul scurt este: uneori. Siguranța apei de ploaie depinde de locul și modul în care este colectată. Apa de ploaie este bună de băut? […] Articolul Test de cultură generală. Apa de ploaie este bună de băut? apare prima dată în Descopera .

romania.summary: Google Pixel 10a este cel mai nou telefon de la Google și deși nu aduce foarte multe noutăți față de modelul anterior este în continuare un telefon excelent pentru cei care vor o experiență Android...

romania.title: Ștefan Ciubotărașu: viața actorului celor 300 de roluri, între scenă, film și poezie

romania.summary: Google schimbă structura taxelor din Play Store și renunță la modelul tradițional de comision de până la 30% pentru aplicațiile Android.

romania.summary: Putem ignora trendurile tehnologice la nivelul societății, putem considera că sunt trecătoare, dar un lucru este sigur: schimbările vor avea loc. Există doar două locuri unde o țară poate fi. În...

romania.summary: Proiectele de energie regenerabilă se dezvoltă rapid în România, iar eficiența energetică devine și ea tot mai importantă. În acest context, soluțiile inovatoare de producție și mai ales de stocare...

romania.title: Bateria cu sodiu care stochează mai multă energie și produce apă potabilă
romania.summary: Cercetători de la Universitatea din Surrey au dezvoltat o baterie sodium-ion care, potrivit rezultatelor publicate, poate stoca aproape de două ori mai multă energie decât variantele convenționale...

Iosif) din Brașov, Rodica Bretin lansează volumul „Pisica de la ora cinci”, apărut în colecția Corint Fiction. … Read More The post Rodica Bretin lansează romanul puzzle „Pisica de la ora cinci” la Librăria Libris din Brașov appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

medical.title: Study finds shared genetic roots of multiple sclerosis across diverse ancestries
medical.summary: A new study published in Neurology, led by Queen Mary University of London, has revealed that people of South Asian, African and European ancestry share many of the same genetic risk factors for multiple sclerosis (MS).

medical.title: Team approach, low-cost interventions lead to major reduction in opioid prescriptions after surgery
medical.summary: A novel team approach founded on a culture change among clinicians and doctor-patient communication successfully reduced post-surgical opioid use, a new study led by University of Rochester Medical Center (URMC) researchers shows.

medical.title: That difficult person in your life could be aging you 1.5% faster, study finds
medical.summary: Spending time with someone who constantly causes problems may do more than just ruin your mood.

medical.title: Mouse study sheds light on how the brain recognizes stable patterns in changing scenes
medical.summary: Humans and many other animals can innately recognize familiar objects in their surroundings, irrespective of the angle they are observed from, changes in lighting or other shifts in the surrounding environment. This ability is known to be supported by a process known as generalization, which allows the brain to respond similarly to different sensory inputs that share some common important features.

medical.title: Study reveals worrying extent of imprecise gene and gene mutation naming
medical.summary: A systematic review of 52 scientific papers submitted to a world-leading clinical genetics journal from multiple scientists over a two-year period reveals that not a single one named critical gene mutations (correctly termed as variants) with precision. The findings partly explain why about 70% of rare diseases go undiagnosed, even in the U.K., which arguably has the world's most advanced genomic medicine service. Led by Dr. Peter Freeman, a geneticist from The University of Manchester, the findings are published in Clinical Chemistry.

medical.title: Immune cells play key role in regulating eye pressure linked to glaucoma, research reveals
medical.summary: When the eye's drainage system clogs, pressure builds up and causes damage. The pressure can lead to glaucoma and vision loss. New research, published in the journal Immunity, reveals that a specialized set of immune cells act as the cleanup crew, pointing to a promising new target for therapies to prevent a major cause of blindness.

medical.title: Biomimetic platform developed to enhance CAR T cell therapy against leukemia
medical.summary: Chimeric antigen receptor T (CAR T) cell therapy represents a milestone in leukemia treatment. CAR T works by genetically engineering a chimeric antigen receptor on the surface of the patient's T cells to target specific antigens on leukemia cells, with the goal of identifying and eliminating them. However, clinical data show that more than 50% of patients eventually relapse after CAR T treatment. One major reason is that leukemia cells can reduce or lose expression of the targeted antigen under therapeutic pressure. When this occurs, CAR T cells can no longer effectively recognize and eliminate leukemia cells.

medical.title: Out-of-state abortion care: Patient interviews reveal delays and stigma
medical.summary: State-level abortion restrictions have shifted the landscape of care and the experiences of people traveling for abortion care after the June 2022 Dobbs v Jackson Women's Health Organization Supreme Court decision. A new, qualitative study published JAMA Network Open takes a deeper look at the experiences of people traveling from U.S. states with abortion restrictions or bans to Illinois, a state where abortion remains legal.

medical.title: Policy change tied to higher kidney transplant rates for Black patients
medical.summary: A new national study evaluating a landmark U.S. transplant policy change finds that efforts to correct the harms of race-based kidney function equations are associated with increased kidney transplantation rates among Black patients. The study, published in JAMA Internal Medicine, underscores how reparative strategies that address the harms of race-based algorithms in medicine can help save lives.

science.title: Scientists just looked inside Darwin’s 200-year-old specimen jars without opening them
science.summary: Scientists have used a laser technique to analyze Charles Darwin’s original Galápagos specimens without opening their nearly 200-year-old jars. By shining light through the glass, the method reveals the chemical makeup of the preservation fluids inside. Researchers successfully identified the contents in most samples, offering new clues about historical preservation practices. The breakthrough could help museums protect millions of delicate specimens without risking damage.

science.title: Half of Amazon insects could face dangerous heat stress
science.summary: A sweeping new study of more than 2,000 insect species reveals a troubling reality: many insects may be far less capable of coping with rising temperatures than scientists once hoped. Researchers found that while some species living at higher altitudes can temporarily boost their heat tolerance, many insects in tropical lowlands—where biodiversity is highest—lack this flexibility. Because insects play essential roles as pollinators, decomposers, and predators, their vulnerability could ripple through entire ecosystems.

science.title: A Little Town with a Long Name
science.summary: A NASA luminary from the Apollo era grew up in Wales near Llanfairpwllgwyngyllgogerychwyrndrobwllllantysiliogogogoch.

science.title: High-Speed Flight Project Overview
science.summary: What We do The High-Speed Flight (HSF) project develops technologies that make high-speed, airbreathing, commercial flight possible from Mach 1 to Mach 5 and above. HSF creates tools, technologies, and knowledge that will help eliminate today’s technical barriers to practical supersonic flight, most notably sonic boom. The project supports the X-59 quiet supersonic vehicle testing […]

science.title: 2700-year-old teeth reveal the hidden lives of Iron Age Italians
science.summary: Iron Age teeth from southern Italy have become time capsules, preserving intimate details of childhood and diet. Growth lines in the enamel reveal moments of early-life stress, while hardened plaque holds microscopic remains of cereals, legumes, and fermented foods. The findings suggest a community with diverse food resources and strong Mediterranean connections. Even a small sample offers a striking glimpse into how people lived, grew, and ate nearly three millennia ago.

science.title: About Advanced Air Vehicles Program (AAVP)
science.summary: NASA’s Advanced Air Vehicles Program (AAVP) studies, evaluates, and develops technologies and capabilities for new aircraft systems and explores far-future concepts for revolutionary air travel improvements. AAVP develops technologies for all flight regimes from hover to hypersonic to enable safe, new aircraft that are faster, quieter, and more fuel efficient. AAVP develops a broad range […]

science.title: Blowing Stellar Bubbles
science.summary: For the first time, a young, Sun-like star has been caught red-handed blowing bubbles in the galaxy, by astronomers using NASA’s Chandra X-ray Observatory. The bubble – called an “astrosphere” – completely surrounds the juvenile star in this image released on Feb. 23, 2026. Winds from the star’s surface are blowing up the bubble and […]

science.title: I Am Artemis: Paul Boehm
science.summary: Listen to this audio excerpt from Paul Boehm, Orion crew support and thermal systems functional area manager: As the Artemis II astronauts fly around the Moon, they’ll rely on systems inside the Orion spacecraft to live, work, and keep them safe during their mission. At NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston, Paul Boehm, crew support and […]

science.title: NASA, OPM Launch NASA Force to Recruit Top Talent for US Space Program
science.summary: The U.S. Office of Personnel Management (OPM) and NASA announced NASA Force on Wednesday, a dedicated talent track within the US Tech Force initiative designed to recruit and deploy the nation’s top engineers and technologists to support America’s space program. NASA Force will identify and place high-impact technical talent into mission-critical roles supporting NASA’s exploration, […]

science.title: US-French Satellite Takes Stock of World’s River Water
science.summary: In a first, a space mission led by NASA and France has tracked Earth’s rivers swelling and shrinking from month to month over the course of a year and found significantly less of a swing than previous model-based estimates. A record drought in the Amazon likely influenced the tally made by the Surface Water and […]

science.title: Searching for Selenite
science.summary: Oklahoma’s Salt Plains National Wildlife Refuge attracts rare and diverse species—and enthusiasts looking for a distinct type of crystallized gypsum.

science.title: Neutrinos could explain why matter survived the Big Bang
science.summary: An international team combining two major neutrino experiments has uncovered stronger evidence that neutrinos and antimatter don’t behave as perfect mirror images. That subtle difference may hold the key to why the universe didn’t vanish in a flash of self-destruction after the Big Bang.

science.title: La NASA refuerza Artemis: añade una misión y perfecciona su arquitectura general
science.summary: Read this story in English here. A fin de lograr el objetivo nacional de llevar astronautas estadounidenses a la superficie de la Luna y mantener la superioridad de Estados Unidos en exploración y descubrimientos, la NASA anunció el 27 de febrero que aumentará la frecuencia de sus misiones con el programa Artemis, estandarizará la configuración del […]

science.title: NASA Strengthens Artemis: Adds Mission, Refines Overall Architecture
science.summary: To achieve the national goal of landing American astronauts on the surface of the Moon and maintaining U.S. superiority in exploration and discovery, NASA announced Feb. 27 it is increasing its cadence of missions under the Artemis program, standardizing the SLS (Space Launch System) rocket configuration, and adding a new mission. The plans were shared […]

science.title: Scientists capture a magnetic flip in 140 trillionths of a second
science.summary: Scientists at the University of Tokyo have captured something never seen before: a frame-by-frame view of how electron spins flip inside an antiferromagnet, a material once thought to be magnetically “invisible.” By firing ultrafast electrical pulses into a thin layer of manganese–tin and tracking the response with precisely timed flashes of light, the team uncovered two distinct switching mechanisms. One relies on heat generated by strong currents, while the other flips spins directly with minimal heating — a far more efficient process.

science.title: Blasted off Mars and still alive
science.summary: A famously resilient bacterium may be tough enough to survive one of the most violent events imaginable on Mars. In laboratory experiments designed to mimic the crushing shock of a massive asteroid impact, researchers squeezed Deinococcus radiodurans between steel plates and blasted it with pressures reaching 3 GPa (30,000 times atmospheric pressure). Even under these extreme conditions, a significant portion of the microbes survived.

science.title: James Webb spots a galaxy with tentacles in deep space
science.summary: Astronomers using the James Webb Space Telescope have spotted the most distant “jellyfish galaxy” ever seen — a cosmic oddity streaming long, tentacle-like trails of gas and newborn stars as it speeds through a dense galaxy cluster. The galaxy appears as it was 8.5 billion years ago, revealing that the early universe may have been far more violent than scientists expected.

science.title: A flash of laser light flips a magnet in major light-control breakthrough
science.summary: Researchers at the University of Basel and the ETH in Zurich have succeeded in changing the polarity of a special ferromagnet using a laser beam. In the future, this method could be used to create adaptable electronic circuits with light.

science.title: For every known vertebrate species, two more may be hiding in plain sight
science.summary: Earth’s vertebrate diversity may be far richer than anyone realized. A sweeping analysis of more than 300 studies suggests that for every known fish, bird, reptile, amphibian, or mammal species, there are about two nearly identical “cryptic” species hiding in plain sight—genetically distinct but visually almost impossible to tell apart. Thanks to advances in DNA sequencing, scientists are uncovering these long-separated lineages, some evolving independently for over a million years.

science.title: Atacama surprise: The world’s driest desert is teeming with hidden life
science.summary: Even in the ultra-dry Atacama Desert, tiny soil-dwelling nematodes are thriving in surprising diversity. Scientists found that biodiversity increases with moisture and altitude shapes which species survive. In the most extreme zones, many nematodes reproduce asexually — a possible survival advantage. The discovery suggests that life in arid regions may be far richer, and more fragile, than once believed.

science.title: Hidden oceans on icy moons may be boiling beneath the surface
science.summary: Icy moons circling the outer planets may be far more dynamic—and explosive—than they appear. New research suggests that when heat from tidal forces melts their ice shells from below, the sudden drop in pressure could cause hidden oceans to boil beneath the surface. On smaller moons like Enceladus, Mimas, and Miranda, this process may help explain strange features such as Enceladus’ tiger stripes and Miranda’s towering cliffs.

science.title: Scientists just turned light into a remote control for crystals
science.summary: NYU researchers have found a way to use light to control how microscopic particles assemble into crystals, effectively turning illumination into a tool for shaping matter. By adding light-sensitive molecules to a liquid filled with tiny particles, they can adjust how strongly the particles attract or repel one another simply by changing the light’s intensity or pattern. This allows them to trigger crystals to form, dissolve, or even be reshaped in real time.

science.title: Massive asteroid impact 6.3 million years ago left giant glass field in Brazil
science.summary: For the first time ever, scientists have uncovered a vast field of tektites in Brazil — mysterious glassy fragments forged when a powerful extraterrestrial object slammed into Earth about 6.3 million years ago. Named “geraisites” after Minas Gerais, where they were first found, these dark, aerodynamic droplets of natural glass stretch across more than 900 kilometers and may mark one of South America’s most significant ancient impact events.

science.title: For the first time, light mimics a Nobel Prize quantum effect
science.summary: Scientists have pulled off a feat long considered out of reach: getting light to mimic the famous quantum Hall effect. In their experiment, photons drift sideways in perfectly defined, quantized steps—just like electrons do in powerful magnetic fields. Because these steps depend only on nature’s fundamental constants, they could become a new gold standard for ultra-precise measurements. The discovery also hints at tougher, more reliable quantum photonic technologies.

science.title: A faint cosmic hum could solve the Universe’s expansion mystery
science.summary: Astronomers have long known the universe is expanding—but exactly how fast remains one of the biggest mysteries in cosmology. Different techniques for measuring the Hubble constant stubbornly disagree, creating the so-called “Hubble tension.” Now researchers at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign and the University of Chicago have unveiled a bold new way to weigh in on the debate using gravitational waves—the faint ripples in spacetime produced by colliding black holes.

environment.title: The hidden technology that could unlock commercial fusion power
environment.summary: Fusion energy may be one of the most promising clean power sources of the future—but only if scientists can precisely measure the extreme, fast-moving plasmas that make it possible. A new U.S. Department of Energy–sponsored report urges major investment in advanced diagnostic tools—the high-tech “sensors” that track plasma temperature, density, and behavior inside fusion systems. Bringing together 70 experts from universities, national labs, and private industry, the workshop identified seven priority areas ranging from burning plasma to full-scale pilot plants.

environment.title: Ancient mystery on K’gari: World’s largest sand island lakes dried up during rainy era
environment.summary: K’gari’s iconic lakes have existed for tens of thousands of years—but they haven’t always been full. New research shows that about 7,500 years ago, during a time of high rainfall, several of the island’s deepest lakes mysteriously vanished. Scientists believe changing wind patterns may have redirected rain away from the island. As the climate shifts again, the lakes’ long-term survival is no longer guaranteed.

environment.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
environment.title: Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars
environment.summary: The hill-shaped features are a sign of explosive volcanic activity—a rarity on the Red Planet. The post Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: A major climate hope in Antarctica just melted away
environment.summary: A popular climate theory suggested that melting Antarctic glaciers would release iron into the ocean, sparking algae blooms that pull carbon dioxide from the air. New field data from West Antarctica reveal that meltwater provides far less iron than scientists once believed. Instead, most of the iron comes from deep ocean water and sediments, not from the melting ice itself. The discovery raises new questions about how Antarctica influences climate change.

environment.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
environment.title: Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice
environment.summary: Nearly 50 years ago, the first Landsat satellite captured the rare sight of Mid-Atlantic waterways frozen over. The post Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Antarctica just saw the fastest glacier collapse ever recorded
environment.summary: Antarctica’s Hektoria Glacier stunned scientists by retreating eight kilometers in just two months, with nearly half of it collapsing in record time. The rapid breakup was driven by a flat, underwater bedrock surface that allowed the glacier to suddenly float and fracture from below. Satellite and seismic data captured the dramatic chain reaction in near real time. The findings raise concerns that much larger glaciers could one day collapse just as quickly.

environment.title: Congo basin blackwater lakes are releasing ancient carbon into the atmosphere
environment.summary: Deep in the Congo Basin, vast peatlands quietly store enormous amounts of Earth’s carbon — but new research suggests this ancient vault may be leaking. Scientists studying Africa’s largest blackwater lakes discovered that significant amounts of carbon dioxide bubbling into the atmosphere come not just from recent plant life, but from peat that has been locked away for thousands of years.

environment.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
environment.title: Showy Swirls Around Jeju Island
environment.summary: Winds blowing past the volcanic landmass near the Korean Peninsula created a trail of spiraling clouds, while murky water churned nearby. The post Showy Swirls Around Jeju Island appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
environment.title: Curiosity Studies Nodules on Boxwork Formations
environment.summary: Description NASA’s Curiosity Mars rover discovered these bumpy, pea-sized nodules while exploring a region filled with boxwork formations — low ridges standing roughly 3 to 6 feet (1 to 2 meters) tall with sandy hollows in-between. This mosaic is made up of 50 individual images taken by Curiosity’s Mars Hand Lens Imager (MAHLI), a camera on […] The post Curiosity Studies Nodules on Boxwork Formations appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Curiosity Surveys the Boxwork Region
environment.summary: Description NASA’s Curiosity Mars rover captured this panorama of boxwork formations — the low ridges seen here with hollows in between them — using its Mastcam on Sept. 26, 2025, the 4,671st Martian day, or sol, of the mission. These boxwork formations were created billions of years ago when water leaked through rock cracks. Minerals […] The post Curiosity Surveys the Boxwork Region appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Schrödinger’s color theory finally completed after 100 years
environment.summary: A century after Erwin Schrödinger sketched out a bold vision for how we perceive color, scientists have finally filled in the missing pieces. A Los Alamos team used advanced geometry to show that hue, saturation, and lightness aren’t shaped by culture or experience — they’re built directly into the mathematical structure of how we see color. By defining a crucial missing element known as the “neutral axis,” the researchers repaired a long-standing flaw in Schrödinger’s model and even corrected tricky visual quirks like the way brightness can subtly shift perceived hue.

environment.title: A giant blade-crested spinosaurus, the “hell heron,” discovered in the Sahara
environment.summary: Deep in the heart of the Sahara, scientists have uncovered Spinosaurus mirabilis — a spectacular new predator crowned with a massive, scimitar-shaped crest that may once have blazed with color under the desert sun. Discovered in remote inland river deposits in Niger, the fossil rewrites what we thought we knew about spinosaur dinosaurs, suggesting they weren’t fully aquatic hunters but powerful waders stalking fish in forested waterways hundreds of miles from the sea.

environment.title: Arctic Blast Brightened the West Florida Shelf
environment.summary: A cold snap in the southern U.S. stirred up a dazzling display of sediment in coastal waters. The post Arctic Blast Brightened the West Florida Shelf appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Triceratops had a giant nose that may have cooled its massive head
environment.summary: Triceratops’ massive head may have been doing more than just showing off those famous horns. Using CT scans and 3D reconstructions of fossil skulls, researchers uncovered a surprisingly complex nasal system hidden inside its enormous snout. Instead of being just a supersized nose for smelling, it likely housed intricate networks of nerves and blood vessels—and even special structures that helped regulate heat and moisture.

environment.title: Frozen for 5,000 years, this ice cave bacterium resists modern antibiotics
environment.summary: Deep inside a Romanian ice cave, locked away in a 5,000-year-old layer of ice, scientists have uncovered a bacterium with a startling secret: it’s resistant to many modern antibiotics. Despite predating the antibiotic era, this cold-loving microbe carries more than 100 resistance-related genes and can survive drugs used today to treat serious infections like tuberculosis and UTIs.

environment.title: Scientists uncover oxygen-loving ancestor of all complex life
environment.summary: For decades, scientists have believed that complex life began when two very different microbes joined forces, eventually giving rise to plants, animals, and fungi. But one major puzzle remained: how could these organisms have met if one depended on oxygen and the other supposedly lived without it? New research suggests the answer lies in ancient microbes called Asgard archaea.

environment.title: Winds Whip Up Fires and Dust on the Southern Plains
environment.summary: Dry, gusty conditions spurred fast-growing fires in Oklahoma and Kansas, along with dangerous dust storms across the region. The post Winds Whip Up Fires and Dust on the Southern Plains appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Giant virus discovery could rewrite the origin of complex life
environment.summary: A giant virus discovered in Japan is adding fuel to the provocative idea that viruses helped create complex life. Named ushikuvirus, it infects amoebae and shows unique traits that connect different families of giant DNA viruses. Its unusual way of hijacking and disrupting the host cell’s nucleus offers fresh insight into how viruses may have influenced the evolution of the cell nucleus itself. The finding deepens the mystery of viruses—and their possible role in life’s biggest leap.

environment.title: Ancient DNA solves 5,500 year old burial mystery in Sweden
environment.summary: Ancient DNA from a Stone Age burial site in Sweden shows that families 5,500 years ago were more complex than expected. Many individuals buried together were not immediate family, but second- or third-degree relatives. One grave held a young woman alongside two children who were siblings—yet she wasn’t their mother. The discoveries hint at tight-knit communities where extended kin mattered deeply.

environment.title: New map reveals where lethal scorpions are most likely to strike
environment.summary: Scientists have developed a powerful new way to forecast where some of the world’s most dangerous scorpions are likely to be found. By combining fieldwork in Africa with advanced computer modeling, the team discovered that soil type is the strongest factor shaping where many lethal species live, while temperature patterns also play a key role.

environment.title: A spinning gyroscope could finally unlock ocean wave energy
environment.summary: Ocean waves are a vast and steady source of renewable energy, but capturing their power efficiently has long frustrated engineers. A researcher at The University of Osaka has now explored a bold new approach: a gyroscopic wave energy converter that uses a spinning flywheel inside a floating structure to turn wave motion into electricity. By harnessing gyroscopic precession—the subtle wobble of a spinning object under force—the system can be tuned to absorb energy across a wide range of wave conditions.

environment.title: Ancient microbes may have used oxygen 500 million years before it filled Earth’s atmosphere
environment.summary: Life on Earth may have learned to breathe oxygen long before oxygen filled the skies. MIT researchers traced a key oxygen-processing enzyme back hundreds of millions of years before the Great Oxidation Event. Early microbes living near oxygen-producing cyanobacteria may have quickly used up the gas as it formed, slowing its rise in the atmosphere. The results suggest life was adapting to oxygen far earlier — and far more creatively — than once thought.

mix_items.title: 😂 Charalambous stă de 4 ore într-un restaurant şi aşteaptă să-i zică Becali ce să comande
mix_items.summary: Ţi se rupe inima când îl vezi pe fostul antrenor al FCSB! Precum un Shiba Inu adorabil care îşi aşteaptă stăpânul, fie ploaie, fie vânt, Elias Charalmambous speră încă să primească nişte indicaţii de la Becali care nu vor mai veni niciodată. Astăzi, Chambarlabous a fost văzut într-un restaurant din Capitală, vizibil înfometat, privind în […] The post Charalambous stă de 4 ore într-un restaurant şi aşteaptă să-i zică Becali ce să comande appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: 😂 Primăvară superbă în București! Au ieșit ghioceii în miile de gropi din asfalt
mix_items.summary: După o iarnă lungă, Bucureștiul prinde din nou viață: păsărelele ciripesc, copacii înmuguresc, iar șoferii trag stânga-dreapta de volan ca să evite „craterele” din asfalt, care au apărut, aparent, peste noapte. Printre ele au răsărit și ghiocei! Singurele flori suficient de rezistente ca să supraviețuiască claxoanelor de taximetriști, sării de deszăpezire și promisiunilor electorale. Specialiștii […] The post Primăvară superbă în București! Au ieșit ghioceii în miile de gropi din asfalt appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: 😂 Românii cu Oltcit răsuflă ușurați! S-au scumpit benzina și motorina, dar împinsul e tot gratis
mix_items.summary: Vestea că românii vor scoate mai mulți bani din buzunar pentru deplasările cu mașina personală a creat panică printre șoferi, inclusiv printre cei care au Oltcit. Aceștia din urmă pot însă să răsufle ușurați: într-adevăr, benzina și motorina se vor scumpi, dar împinsul Oltcitului va rămâne în continuare gratuit. Chiar și așa, românii cu Oltcit […] The post Românii cu Oltcit răsuflă ușurați! S-au scumpit benzina și motorina, dar împinsul e tot gratis appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Premiile Excelenței Interpretative – Gala UCIMR 2026
mix_items.summary: Uniunea de Creație Interpretativă a Muzicienilor din România (UCIMR) anunță Premiile Excelenței Interpretative – Gala UCIMR 2026 – aflată la a II-a ediție, ce va avea loc marți, 24 martie… Articolul Premiile Excelenței Interpretative – Gala UCIMR 2026 apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: S-au pus în vânzare biletele pentru Festivalul Filmului Francez .30
mix_items.summary: Filme de la Cannes, Berlin, Locarno, Veneția și producții premiate cu César vor fi prezentate în selecția Festivalului Filmului Francez 2026 Programul complet al ediției aniversare poate fi descoperit pe:… Articolul S-au pus în vânzare biletele pentru Festivalul Filmului Francez .30 apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Inteligența implică o coordonare a întregului creier, dezvăluie un studiu
mix_items.summary: Cercetătorii de la Universitatea Notre Dame sugerează că inteligența nu locuiește deloc într-o singură regiune „inteligentă” a creierului. În schimb, ea emerge din modul eficient și flexibil în care numeroasele rețele ale creierului comunică și […] Articolul Inteligența implică o coordonare a întregului creier, dezvăluie un studiu apare prima dată în Descopera .

mix_items.title: Test de cultură generală. Apa de ploaie este bună de băut?
mix_items.summary: Te-ai întrebat vreodată dacă apa de ploaie este bună de băut? Răspunsul scurt este: uneori. Siguranța apei de ploaie depinde de locul și modul în care este colectată. Apa de ploaie este bună de băut? […] Articolul Test de cultură generală. Apa de ploaie este bună de băut? apare prima dată în Descopera .

mix_items.title: REVIEW Google Pixel 10a: singurul Android pentru care aș renunța la iPhone
mix_items.summary: Google Pixel 10a este cel mai nou telefon de la Google și deși nu aduce foarte multe noutăți față de modelul anterior este în continuare un telefon excelent pentru cei care vor o experiență Android...

mix_items.title: Ștefan Ciubotărașu: viața actorului celor 300 de roluri, între scenă, film și poezie
mix_items.summary: Ștefan Ciubotărașu rămâne un reper de echilibru și umanitate. Un actor care nu a jucat doar roluri, a trăit prin ele. Articolul Ștefan Ciubotărașu: viața actorului celor 300 de roluri, între scenă, film și poezie apare prima dată în life.ro .

mix_items.title: Google schimbă modelul de comisioane din Play Store după disputa cu Epic Games
mix_items.summary: Google schimbă structura taxelor din Play Store și renunță la modelul tradițional de comision de până la 30% pentru aplicațiile Android.

mix_items.title: Marea divergență adusă de AI - de care parte a baricadei vrem să fim?
mix_items.summary: Putem ignora trendurile tehnologice la nivelul societății, putem considera că sunt trecătoare, dar un lucru este sigur: schimbările vor avea loc. Există doar două locuri unde o țară poate fi. În...

mix_items.title: Genuin, afacere de familie cu așternuturi sustenabile, se extinde în Europa
mix_items.summary: Genuin, brandul românesc specializat în produse home & deco realizate exclusiv din in 100% european, anunță accelerarea planurilor de extindere internațională, cu sprijinul Băncii Europene pentru...

mix_items.title: Study finds shared genetic roots of multiple sclerosis across diverse ancestries
mix_items.summary: A new study published in Neurology, led by Queen Mary University of London, has revealed that people of South Asian, African and European ancestry share many of the same genetic risk factors for multiple sclerosis (MS).

mix_items.title: Compania din Târgu Mureș care vrea să cucerească eficiența energetică în Europa Centrală
mix_items.summary: Proiectele de energie regenerabilă se dezvoltă rapid în România, iar eficiența energetică devine și ea tot mai importantă. În acest context, soluțiile inovatoare de producție și mai ales de stocare...

mix_items.title: Bateria cu sodiu care stochează mai multă energie și produce apă potabilă
mix_items.summary: Cercetători de la Universitatea din Surrey au dezvoltat o baterie sodium-ion care, potrivit rezultatelor publicate, poate stoca aproape de două ori mai multă energie decât variantele convenționale...

mix_items.title: Team approach, low-cost interventions lead to major reduction in opioid prescriptions after surgery
mix_items.summary: A novel team approach founded on a culture change among clinicians and doctor-patient communication successfully reduced post-surgical opioid use, a new study led by University of Rochester Medical Center (URMC) researchers shows.

mix_items.title: Rodica Bretin lansează romanul puzzle „Pisica de la ora cinci” la Librăria Libris din Brașov
mix_items.summary: Joi, 12 februarie, de la ora 18:00, la Librăria Libris (Str. Șt. O. Iosif) din Brașov, Rodica Bretin lansează volumul „Pisica de la ora cinci”, apărut în colecția Corint Fiction. … Read More The post Rodica Bretin lansează romanul puzzle „Pisica de la ora cinci” la Librăria Libris din Brașov appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.title: That difficult person in your life could be aging you 1.5% faster, study finds
mix_items.summary: Spending time with someone who constantly causes problems may do more than just ruin your mood.

mix_items.title: Mouse study sheds light on how the brain recognizes stable patterns in changing scenes
mix_items.summary: Humans and many other animals can innately recognize familiar objects in their surroundings, irrespective of the angle they are observed from, changes in lighting or other shifts in the surrounding environment. This ability is known to be supported by a process known as generalization, which allows the brain to respond similarly to different sensory inputs that share some common important features.

mix_items.title: Study reveals worrying extent of imprecise gene and gene mutation naming
mix_items.summary: A systematic review of 52 scientific papers submitted to a world-leading clinical genetics journal from multiple scientists over a two-year period reveals that not a single one named critical gene mutations (correctly termed as variants) with precision. The findings partly explain why about 70% of rare diseases go undiagnosed, even in the U.K., which arguably has the world's most advanced genomic medicine service. Led by Dr. Peter Freeman, a geneticist from The University of Manchester, the findings are published in Clinical Chemistry.

mix_items.title: Immune cells play key role in regulating eye pressure linked to glaucoma, research reveals
mix_items.summary: When the eye's drainage system clogs, pressure builds up and causes damage. The pressure can lead to glaucoma and vision loss. New research, published in the journal Immunity, reveals that a specialized set of immune cells act as the cleanup crew, pointing to a promising new target for therapies to prevent a major cause of blindness.

mix_items.title: Biomimetic platform developed to enhance CAR T cell therapy against leukemia
mix_items.summary: Chimeric antigen receptor T (CAR T) cell therapy represents a milestone in leukemia treatment. CAR T works by genetically engineering a chimeric antigen receptor on the surface of the patient's T cells to target specific antigens on leukemia cells, with the goal of identifying and eliminating them. However, clinical data show that more than 50% of patients eventually relapse after CAR T treatment. One major reason is that leukemia cells can reduce or lose expression of the targeted antigen under therapeutic pressure. When this occurs, CAR T cells can no longer effectively recognize and eliminate leukemia cells.

mix_items.title: Out-of-state abortion care: Patient interviews reveal delays and stigma
mix_items.summary: State-level abortion restrictions have shifted the landscape of care and the experiences of people traveling for abortion care after the June 2022 Dobbs v Jackson Women's Health Organization Supreme Court decision. A new, qualitative study published JAMA Network Open takes a deeper look at the experiences of people traveling from U.S. states with abortion restrictions or bans to Illinois, a state where abortion remains legal.

mix_items.title: Policy change tied to higher kidney transplant rates for Black patients
mix_items.summary: A new national study evaluating a landmark U.S. transplant policy change finds that efforts to correct the harms of race-based kidney function equations are associated with increased kidney transplantation rates among Black patients. The study, published in JAMA Internal Medicine, underscores how reparative strategies that address the harms of race-based algorithms in medicine can help save lives.

mix_items.title: Scientists just looked inside Darwin’s 200-year-old specimen jars without opening them
mix_items.summary: Scientists have used a laser technique to analyze Charles Darwin’s original Galápagos specimens without opening their nearly 200-year-old jars. By shining light through the glass, the method reveals the chemical makeup of the preservation fluids inside. Researchers successfully identified the contents in most samples, offering new clues about historical preservation practices. The breakthrough could help museums protect millions of delicate specimens without risking damage.

mix_items.title: Half of Amazon insects could face dangerous heat stress
mix_items.summary: A sweeping new study of more than 2,000 insect species reveals a troubling reality: many insects may be far less capable of coping with rising temperatures than scientists once hoped. Researchers found that while some species living at higher altitudes can temporarily boost their heat tolerance, many insects in tropical lowlands—where biodiversity is highest—lack this flexibility. Because insects play essential roles as pollinators, decomposers, and predators, their vulnerability could ripple through entire ecosystems.

mix_items.title: A Little Town with a Long Name
mix_items.summary: A NASA luminary from the Apollo era grew up in Wales near Llanfairpwllgwyngyllgogerychwyrndrobwllllantysiliogogogoch.

mix_items.title: High-Speed Flight Project Overview
mix_items.summary: What We do The High-Speed Flight (HSF) project develops technologies that make high-speed, airbreathing, commercial flight possible from Mach 1 to Mach 5 and above. HSF creates tools, technologies, and knowledge that will help eliminate today’s technical barriers to practical supersonic flight, most notably sonic boom. The project supports the X-59 quiet supersonic vehicle testing […]

mix_items.title: 2700-year-old teeth reveal the hidden lives of Iron Age Italians
mix_items.summary: Iron Age teeth from southern Italy have become time capsules, preserving intimate details of childhood and diet. Growth lines in the enamel reveal moments of early-life stress, while hardened plaque holds microscopic remains of cereals, legumes, and fermented foods. The findings suggest a community with diverse food resources and strong Mediterranean connections. Even a small sample offers a striking glimpse into how people lived, grew, and ate nearly three millennia ago.

mix_items.title: About Advanced Air Vehicles Program (AAVP)
mix_items.summary: NASA’s Advanced Air Vehicles Program (AAVP) studies, evaluates, and develops technologies and capabilities for new aircraft systems and explores far-future concepts for revolutionary air travel improvements. AAVP develops technologies for all flight regimes from hover to hypersonic to enable safe, new aircraft that are faster, quieter, and more fuel efficient. AAVP develops a broad range […]

mix_items.title: Blowing Stellar Bubbles
mix_items.summary: For the first time, a young, Sun-like star has been caught red-handed blowing bubbles in the galaxy, by astronomers using NASA’s Chandra X-ray Observatory. The bubble – called an “astrosphere” – completely surrounds the juvenile star in this image released on Feb. 23, 2026. Winds from the star’s surface are blowing up the bubble and […]

mix_items.title: I Am Artemis: Paul Boehm
mix_items.summary: Listen to this audio excerpt from Paul Boehm, Orion crew support and thermal systems functional area manager: As the Artemis II astronauts fly around the Moon, they’ll rely on systems inside the Orion spacecraft to live, work, and keep them safe during their mission. At NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston, Paul Boehm, crew support and […]

mix_items.title: NASA, OPM Launch NASA Force to Recruit Top Talent for US Space Program
mix_items.summary: The U.S. Office of Personnel Management (OPM) and NASA announced NASA Force on Wednesday, a dedicated talent track within the US Tech Force initiative designed to recruit and deploy the nation’s top engineers and technologists to support America’s space program. NASA Force will identify and place high-impact technical talent into mission-critical roles supporting NASA’s exploration, […]

mix_items.title: US-French Satellite Takes Stock of World’s River Water
mix_items.summary: In a first, a space mission led by NASA and France has tracked Earth’s rivers swelling and shrinking from month to month over the course of a year and found significantly less of a swing than previous model-based estimates. A record drought in the Amazon likely influenced the tally made by the Surface Water and […]

mix_items.title: Searching for Selenite
mix_items.summary: Oklahoma’s Salt Plains National Wildlife Refuge attracts rare and diverse species—and enthusiasts looking for a distinct type of crystallized gypsum.

mix_items.title: Neutrinos could explain why matter survived the Big Bang
mix_items.summary: An international team combining two major neutrino experiments has uncovered stronger evidence that neutrinos and antimatter don’t behave as perfect mirror images. That subtle difference may hold the key to why the universe didn’t vanish in a flash of self-destruction after the Big Bang.

mix_items.title: La NASA refuerza Artemis: añade una misión y perfecciona su arquitectura general
mix_items.summary: Read this story in English here. A fin de lograr el objetivo nacional de llevar astronautas estadounidenses a la superficie de la Luna y mantener la superioridad de Estados Unidos en exploración y descubrimientos, la NASA anunció el 27 de febrero que aumentará la frecuencia de sus misiones con el programa Artemis, estandarizará la configuración del […]

mix_items.title: NASA Strengthens Artemis: Adds Mission, Refines Overall Architecture
mix_items.summary: To achieve the national goal of landing American astronauts on the surface of the Moon and maintaining U.S. superiority in exploration and discovery, NASA announced Feb. 27 it is increasing its cadence of missions under the Artemis program, standardizing the SLS (Space Launch System) rocket configuration, and adding a new mission. The plans were shared […]

mix_items.title: Scientists capture a magnetic flip in 140 trillionths of a second
mix_items.summary: Scientists at the University of Tokyo have captured something never seen before: a frame-by-frame view of how electron spins flip inside an antiferromagnet, a material once thought to be magnetically “invisible.” By firing ultrafast electrical pulses into a thin layer of manganese–tin and tracking the response with precisely timed flashes of light, the team uncovered two distinct switching mechanisms. One relies on heat generated by strong currents, while the other flips spins directly with minimal heating — a far more efficient process.

mix_items.title: Blasted off Mars and still alive
mix_items.summary: A famously resilient bacterium may be tough enough to survive one of the most violent events imaginable on Mars. In laboratory experiments designed to mimic the crushing shock of a massive asteroid impact, researchers squeezed Deinococcus radiodurans between steel plates and blasted it with pressures reaching 3 GPa (30,000 times atmospheric pressure). Even under these extreme conditions, a significant portion of the microbes survived.

mix_items.title: James Webb spots a galaxy with tentacles in deep space
mix_items.summary: Astronomers using the James Webb Space Telescope have spotted the most distant “jellyfish galaxy” ever seen — a cosmic oddity streaming long, tentacle-like trails of gas and newborn stars as it speeds through a dense galaxy cluster. The galaxy appears as it was 8.5 billion years ago, revealing that the early universe may have been far more violent than scientists expected.

mix_items.title: A flash of laser light flips a magnet in major light-control breakthrough
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the University of Basel and the ETH in Zurich have succeeded in changing the polarity of a special ferromagnet using a laser beam. In the future, this method could be used to create adaptable electronic circuits with light.

mix_items.title: The hidden technology that could unlock commercial fusion power
mix_items.summary: Fusion energy may be one of the most promising clean power sources of the future—but only if scientists can precisely measure the extreme, fast-moving plasmas that make it possible. A new U.S. Department of Energy–sponsored report urges major investment in advanced diagnostic tools—the high-tech “sensors” that track plasma temperature, density, and behavior inside fusion systems. Bringing together 70 experts from universities, national labs, and private industry, the workshop identified seven priority areas ranging from burning plasma to full-scale pilot plants.

mix_items.title: For every known vertebrate species, two more may be hiding in plain sight
mix_items.summary: Earth’s vertebrate diversity may be far richer than anyone realized. A sweeping analysis of more than 300 studies suggests that for every known fish, bird, reptile, amphibian, or mammal species, there are about two nearly identical “cryptic” species hiding in plain sight—genetically distinct but visually almost impossible to tell apart. Thanks to advances in DNA sequencing, scientists are uncovering these long-separated lineages, some evolving independently for over a million years.

mix_items.title: Ancient mystery on K’gari: World’s largest sand island lakes dried up during rainy era
mix_items.summary: K’gari’s iconic lakes have existed for tens of thousands of years—but they haven’t always been full. New research shows that about 7,500 years ago, during a time of high rainfall, several of the island’s deepest lakes mysteriously vanished. Scientists believe changing wind patterns may have redirected rain away from the island. As the climate shifts again, the lakes’ long-term survival is no longer guaranteed.

mix_items.title: Atacama surprise: The world’s driest desert is teeming with hidden life
mix_items.summary: Even in the ultra-dry Atacama Desert, tiny soil-dwelling nematodes are thriving in surprising diversity. Scientists found that biodiversity increases with moisture and altitude shapes which species survive. In the most extreme zones, many nematodes reproduce asexually — a possible survival advantage. The discovery suggests that life in arid regions may be far richer, and more fragile, than once believed.

mix_items.title: Hidden oceans on icy moons may be boiling beneath the surface
mix_items.summary: Icy moons circling the outer planets may be far more dynamic—and explosive—than they appear. New research suggests that when heat from tidal forces melts their ice shells from below, the sudden drop in pressure could cause hidden oceans to boil beneath the surface. On smaller moons like Enceladus, Mimas, and Miranda, this process may help explain strange features such as Enceladus’ tiger stripes and Miranda’s towering cliffs.

mix_items.title: Scientists just turned light into a remote control for crystals
mix_items.summary: NYU researchers have found a way to use light to control how microscopic particles assemble into crystals, effectively turning illumination into a tool for shaping matter. By adding light-sensitive molecules to a liquid filled with tiny particles, they can adjust how strongly the particles attract or repel one another simply by changing the light’s intensity or pattern. This allows them to trigger crystals to form, dissolve, or even be reshaped in real time.

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars
mix_items.summary: The hill-shaped features are a sign of explosive volcanic activity—a rarity on the Red Planet. The post Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Massive asteroid impact 6.3 million years ago left giant glass field in Brazil
mix_items.summary: For the first time ever, scientists have uncovered a vast field of tektites in Brazil — mysterious glassy fragments forged when a powerful extraterrestrial object slammed into Earth about 6.3 million years ago. Named “geraisites” after Minas Gerais, where they were first found, these dark, aerodynamic droplets of natural glass stretch across more than 900 kilometers and may mark one of South America’s most significant ancient impact events.

mix_items.title: For the first time, light mimics a Nobel Prize quantum effect
mix_items.summary: Scientists have pulled off a feat long considered out of reach: getting light to mimic the famous quantum Hall effect. In their experiment, photons drift sideways in perfectly defined, quantized steps—just like electrons do in powerful magnetic fields. Because these steps depend only on nature’s fundamental constants, they could become a new gold standard for ultra-precise measurements. The discovery also hints at tougher, more reliable quantum photonic technologies.

mix_items.title: A faint cosmic hum could solve the Universe’s expansion mystery
mix_items.summary: Astronomers have long known the universe is expanding—but exactly how fast remains one of the biggest mysteries in cosmology. Different techniques for measuring the Hubble constant stubbornly disagree, creating the so-called “Hubble tension.” Now researchers at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign and the University of Chicago have unveiled a bold new way to weigh in on the debate using gravitational waves—the faint ripples in spacetime produced by colliding black holes.

mix_items.title: A major climate hope in Antarctica just melted away
mix_items.summary: A popular climate theory suggested that melting Antarctic glaciers would release iron into the ocean, sparking algae blooms that pull carbon dioxide from the air. New field data from West Antarctica reveal that meltwater provides far less iron than scientists once believed. Instead, most of the iron comes from deep ocean water and sediments, not from the melting ice itself. The discovery raises new questions about how Antarctica influences climate change.

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice
mix_items.summary: Nearly 50 years ago, the first Landsat satellite captured the rare sight of Mid-Atlantic waterways frozen over. The post Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Antarctica just saw the fastest glacier collapse ever recorded
mix_items.summary: Antarctica’s Hektoria Glacier stunned scientists by retreating eight kilometers in just two months, with nearly half of it collapsing in record time. The rapid breakup was driven by a flat, underwater bedrock surface that allowed the glacier to suddenly float and fracture from below. Satellite and seismic data captured the dramatic chain reaction in near real time. The findings raise concerns that much larger glaciers could one day collapse just as quickly.

mix_items.title: Congo basin blackwater lakes are releasing ancient carbon into the atmosphere
mix_items.summary: Deep in the Congo Basin, vast peatlands quietly store enormous amounts of Earth’s carbon — but new research suggests this ancient vault may be leaking. Scientists studying Africa’s largest blackwater lakes discovered that significant amounts of carbon dioxide bubbling into the atmosphere come not just from recent plant life, but from peat that has been locked away for thousands of years.

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: Showy Swirls Around Jeju Island
mix_items.summary: Winds blowing past the volcanic landmass near the Korean Peninsula created a trail of spiraling clouds, while murky water churned nearby. The post Showy Swirls Around Jeju Island appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: Curiosity Studies Nodules on Boxwork Formations
mix_items.summary: Description NASA’s Curiosity Mars rover discovered these bumpy, pea-sized nodules while exploring a region filled with boxwork formations — low ridges standing roughly 3 to 6 feet (1 to 2 meters) tall with sandy hollows in-between. This mosaic is made up of 50 individual images taken by Curiosity’s Mars Hand Lens Imager (MAHLI), a camera on […] The post Curiosity Studies Nodules on Boxwork Formations appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Curiosity Surveys the Boxwork Region
mix_items.summary: Description NASA’s Curiosity Mars rover captured this panorama of boxwork formations — the low ridges seen here with hollows in between them — using its Mastcam on Sept. 26, 2025, the 4,671st Martian day, or sol, of the mission. These boxwork formations were created billions of years ago when water leaked through rock cracks. Minerals […] The post Curiosity Surveys the Boxwork Region appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Schrödinger’s color theory finally completed after 100 years
mix_items.summary: A century after Erwin Schrödinger sketched out a bold vision for how we perceive color, scientists have finally filled in the missing pieces. A Los Alamos team used advanced geometry to show that hue, saturation, and lightness aren’t shaped by culture or experience — they’re built directly into the mathematical structure of how we see color. By defining a crucial missing element known as the “neutral axis,” the researchers repaired a long-standing flaw in Schrödinger’s model and even corrected tricky visual quirks like the way brightness can subtly shift perceived hue.

mix_items.title: A giant blade-crested spinosaurus, the “hell heron,” discovered in the Sahara
mix_items.summary: Deep in the heart of the Sahara, scientists have uncovered Spinosaurus mirabilis — a spectacular new predator crowned with a massive, scimitar-shaped crest that may once have blazed with color under the desert sun. Discovered in remote inland river deposits in Niger, the fossil rewrites what we thought we knew about spinosaur dinosaurs, suggesting they weren’t fully aquatic hunters but powerful waders stalking fish in forested waterways hundreds of miles from the sea.

mix_items.title: Arctic Blast Brightened the West Florida Shelf
mix_items.summary: A cold snap in the southern U.S. stirred up a dazzling display of sediment in coastal waters. The post Arctic Blast Brightened the West Florida Shelf appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Triceratops had a giant nose that may have cooled its massive head
mix_items.summary: Triceratops’ massive head may have been doing more than just showing off those famous horns. Using CT scans and 3D reconstructions of fossil skulls, researchers uncovered a surprisingly complex nasal system hidden inside its enormous snout. Instead of being just a supersized nose for smelling, it likely housed intricate networks of nerves and blood vessels—and even special structures that helped regulate heat and moisture.

mix_items.title: Frozen for 5,000 years, this ice cave bacterium resists modern antibiotics
mix_items.summary: Deep inside a Romanian ice cave, locked away in a 5,000-year-old layer of ice, scientists have uncovered a bacterium with a startling secret: it’s resistant to many modern antibiotics. Despite predating the antibiotic era, this cold-loving microbe carries more than 100 resistance-related genes and can survive drugs used today to treat serious infections like tuberculosis and UTIs.

mix_items.title: Scientists uncover oxygen-loving ancestor of all complex life
mix_items.summary: For decades, scientists have believed that complex life began when two very different microbes joined forces, eventually giving rise to plants, animals, and fungi. But one major puzzle remained: how could these organisms have met if one depended on oxygen and the other supposedly lived without it? New research suggests the answer lies in ancient microbes called Asgard archaea.

mix_items.title: Winds Whip Up Fires and Dust on the Southern Plains
mix_items.summary: Dry, gusty conditions spurred fast-growing fires in Oklahoma and Kansas, along with dangerous dust storms across the region. The post Winds Whip Up Fires and Dust on the Southern Plains appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Giant virus discovery could rewrite the origin of complex life
mix_items.summary: A giant virus discovered in Japan is adding fuel to the provocative idea that viruses helped create complex life. Named ushikuvirus, it infects amoebae and shows unique traits that connect different families of giant DNA viruses. Its unusual way of hijacking and disrupting the host cell’s nucleus offers fresh insight into how viruses may have influenced the evolution of the cell nucleus itself. The finding deepens the mystery of viruses—and their possible role in life’s biggest leap.

mix_items.title: Ancient DNA solves 5,500 year old burial mystery in Sweden
mix_items.summary: Ancient DNA from a Stone Age burial site in Sweden shows that families 5,500 years ago were more complex than expected. Many individuals buried together were not immediate family, but second- or third-degree relatives. One grave held a young woman alongside two children who were siblings—yet she wasn’t their mother. The discoveries hint at tight-knit communities where extended kin mattered deeply.

mix_items.title: New map reveals where lethal scorpions are most likely to strike
mix_items.summary: Scientists have developed a powerful new way to forecast where some of the world’s most dangerous scorpions are likely to be found. By combining fieldwork in Africa with advanced computer modeling, the team discovered that soil type is the strongest factor shaping where many lethal species live, while temperature patterns also play a key role.

mix_items.title: A spinning gyroscope could finally unlock ocean wave energy
mix_items.summary: Ocean waves are a vast and steady source of renewable energy, but capturing their power efficiently has long frustrated engineers. A researcher at The University of Osaka has now explored a bold new approach: a gyroscopic wave energy converter that uses a spinning flywheel inside a floating structure to turn wave motion into electricity. By harnessing gyroscopic precession—the subtle wobble of a spinning object under force—the system can be tuned to absorb energy across a wide range of wave conditions.

mix_items.title: Ancient microbes may have used oxygen 500 million years before it filled Earth’s atmosphere
mix_items.summary: Life on Earth may have learned to breathe oxygen long before oxygen filled the skies. MIT researchers traced a key oxygen-processing enzyme back hundreds of millions of years before the Great Oxidation Event. Early microbes living near oxygen-producing cyanobacteria may have quickly used up the gas as it formed, slowing its rise in the atmosphere. The results suggest life was adapting to oxygen far earlier — and far more creatively — than once thought.

joke_ro.text: - Am zis ca fac sport. Am facut: am fugit de responsabilitati.

satire_ro.title: Charalambous stă de 4 ore într-un restaurant şi aşteaptă să-i zică Becali ce să comande
